article_meta.title: Supreme Court ruling against Trump’s tariffs is unlikely to mean an end to trade policy chaos 1 of 3 | Containers are st

reasoning: The model does an excellent job identifying genuine bias patterns in the author's own prose. All flagged instances are from the author's connecting prose, not from quoted sources. The bias types are reasonable, though several could be more precisely labeled. No attribution rule violations occur. The model catches the most significant instances of loaded language.

custom_reasoning.attribution_rule_compliance: All six flagged instances are from the author's own prose. No quoted text or attributed source language was flagged. Perfect compliance with the attribution rule.
custom_reasoning.false_positive_rate: All flagged items are defensible. 'stunning rebuke,' 'paralyzed,' 'strong-armed,' 'chaotic/unpredictability,' 'lopsided,' and 'squeeze' are all genuinely loaded word choices in the author's voice. Perhaps 'chaotic' could be seen as descriptive given the factual inconsistencies described, but overall these are solid identifications.
custom_reasoning.false_negative_rate: The model caught most major instances. It could have flagged 'sharp-elbowed tactics' as another loaded author characterization, or 'gives the United States a handy cudgel with which to smack countries' as colorful/loaded language. The word 'defeat' in 'his defeat Friday at the Supreme Court' could also be flagged. Minor misses but not critical.
custom_reasoning.bias_type_accuracy: Most items are labeled as 'Spin,' which is defensible but not always the most precise label. 'Stunning' and 'lopsided' could more precisely be 'Subjective Qualifying Adjectives.' 'Paralyzed,' 'strong-armed,' and 'squeeze' could be 'Word Choice.' The labels aren't wrong per se, as Spin covers interpretive language, but more specific labels would be more accurate.
